topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the hairline fracture on the casing with a fingertip, the cool metal a strange anchor in the buzzing workshop. He blinked, a slow, deliberate motion, and looked up at the adjacent workbench. There, bathed in the same harsh fluorescent light, sat a device precisely mirroring his own—the same intricate wiring, the same unique etching on the chassis. A breath hitched in his throat, sharp and sudden. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on concrete sounding deafeningly loud in the sudden silence. He approached the other machine, his movements stiff, his eyes darting from one component to the next, cataloging every identical screw, every minuscule deviation. "Impossible," he whispered, the sound thin and reedy. His hands trembled slightly as he reached out, hovering just above the shared surface, as if expecting the reality to dissolve.

[story 2]

The scent of ozone and solder hung heavy in the air of the subterranean lab. Clara leaned back, her gaze fixed on the schematic spread across the table before her. Then, her peripheral vision snagged on the adjacent station. Another workbench, another identical prototype humming with the same low thrum. A strange stillness settled over her, a sudden vacuum where noise used to be. She pushed her glasses up the bridge of her nose, her jaw tightening. She walked over, her footsteps unnaturally quiet on the polished floor. She ran a hand over the smooth, cool surface of the second device. Her fingers curled into a loose fist, the knuckles whitening. "No," she murmured, the word barely audible, a strange tremor running through her voice. She stepped back abruptly, needing distance from the overwhelming symmetry before she could process what her eyes were telling her.

[story 3]

Marcus stared. His mouth hung slightly open, the usual focused intensity draining away, leaving behind a wide-eyed vacancy. The object before him was a perfect duplicate of the one he had just finished assembling—the same delicate array of crystalline conduits, the identical calibration marks. He took a staggered step backward, bumping against a stack of empty toolboxes. His breathing became shallow, quick puffs of air escaping his lips. He slowly lowered his gaze from the gleaming hardware to the other station, his expression unreadable, yet conveying a profound sense of disorientation. He reached out tentatively, his fingertips brushing the casing of the twin project, a hesitant touch that seemed to confirm a deeply unsettling realization blooming rapidly in his chest.
